skill.name: finishing-a-development-branch
skill.description: Guides a completed development branch through test verification, merge or pull request options, and controlled cleanup.
skill.summary: Finish a development branch with tests, structured integration choices, and safe cleanup.
skill.author: DYAI2025

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command patterns, but the reviewed file is a workflow guide that documents expected git, test, and GitHub CLI commands. The confirmed risk is operational: users could merge, push, delete a branch, or remove a worktree if they follow the commands on the wrong repository or branch. The weak cryptography and device file detections are false positives from text matching and stderr redirection.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Git Cleanup Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The workflow includes branch deletion and worktree removal commands. The skill requires typed confirmation for discard, but these commands can delete local work if used on the wrong branch or path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly documents git branch deletion and worktree removal. The surrounding instructions reduce risk with confirmation, but the operational impact is real.

medium_findings.title: External Repository State Changes
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The skill instructs running test commands, checking out branches, pulling, merging, pushing, and creating a pull request. These commands are legitimate for this skill, but they modify repository state and remote branches.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in code blocks and align with the branch-finishing purpose. No malicious intent or data exfiltration was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detection Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The high static finding points to the YAML description line. No cryptographic API, hashing call, encryption call, or weak algorithm use is present there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is plain metadata text describing the skill. I found no evidence of cryptographic implementation or weak algorithm usage.

low_findings.title: Device File Access Is Stderr Redirection
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The filesystem finding is caused by redirecting git merge-base errors to /dev/null. This is a common shell pattern to suppress expected stderr output.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only device file reference is 2>/dev/null in a git command example. It does not read sensitive files or write persistent data.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Substitution Is Template Usage
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The command substitution examples create a pull request body and inspect the current branch name. They do not execute user-supplied script content, but users should still review generated command text before running it.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The snippets use standard shell constructs with local git and cat commands. Legitimate use is clear, though unreviewed shell execution still carries minor operational risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Blocks
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains shell command blocks for tests, git operations, GitHub CLI pull request creation, branch deletion, and worktree removal. These are expected for a devops workflow but should be executed only after repository state is verified.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command blocks are explicit and numerous. Their purpose is legitimate, but they can change local and remote repository state.

content.value_statement: Finishing a branch can create confusion about tests, merges, pull requests, and cleanup. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a clear workflow for choosing and executing the right integration path.
actual_capabilities: Verifies that tests pass before presenting branch completion options.
Identifies a likely base branch using common git merge-base checks.
Presents four clear choices: merge locally, create a pull request, keep the branch, or discard it.
Provides command sequences for local merge, push, pull request creation, and cleanup.
Requires explicit typed confirmation before destructive discard actions.
Defines when to preserve or remove a git worktree.
limitations: It does not detect every project-specific test command automatically.
It relies on the user or agent to verify the correct base branch.
It can affect local and remote git state if commands are executed incorrectly.
It does not replace repository permissions, branch protection, or code review.

use_cases.title: Complete a feature branch
use_cases.description: A developer can confirm tests, choose a merge path, and clean up the branch after implementation.

use_cases.title: Prepare a pull request
use_cases.description: A team member can push a finished branch and create a pull request with a summary and test plan.

use_cases.title: Manage worktree cleanup
use_cases.description: A maintainer can remove temporary branch worktrees only when the selected workflow makes cleanup appropriate.

prompt_templates.title: Finish this branch
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the finishing-a-development-branch workflow. Verify tests first, then show me the available completion options.

prompt_templates.title: Create a pull request
prompt_templates.scenario: Finished work should be reviewed before merge
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to finish my current branch as a pull request. Include a concise summary and a test plan.

prompt_templates.title: Merge locally with cleanup
prompt_templates.scenario: Work is ready to merge into the base branch
prompt_templates.prompt: Finish this development branch by verifying tests, confirming the base branch, merging locally, and cleaning up only if the merged tests pass.

prompt_templates.title: Audit branch finish options
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review before changing repository state
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the finishing branch workflow, but pause before every command that changes git state. Explain the expected effect and ask for confirmation.

output_examples.input: The implementation is done. What should I do now?
output_examples.output: The assistant verifies tests first. If they pass, it presents four options: merge locally, create a pull request, keep the branch, or discard the work.

output_examples.input: Push this finished branch and open a PR.
output_examples.output: The assistant pushes the branch, drafts a pull request summary and test plan, then preserves the worktree for continued review.
The assistant lists what will be deleted and requires the exact confirmation word before deleting the branch or removing the worktree.

best_practices: Run the real project test command before choosing any completion option.
Confirm the base branch and current branch before merge, push, delete, or cleanup commands.
Review every generated git command before executing it in a repository with valuable work.
anti_patterns: Do not merge or create a pull request when tests are failing.
Do not delete a branch or worktree without explicit typed confirmation.
Do not use this workflow to bypass branch protection or required code review.

faq.question: Does this skill merge code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It presents choices and command guidance. The user should confirm actions that change repository state.

faq.question: Can it create a pull request?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a GitHub CLI pull request flow with a summary and test plan template.

faq.question: Does it require tests to pass?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow stops before completion options if tests fail.

faq.question: Can it delete local work?
faq.answer: Yes, if the discard option is confirmed. The skill requires typed confirmation before branch deletion.

faq.question: Does it support git worktrees?
faq.answer: Yes. It defines when to preserve or remove a worktree after a branch completion choice.

faq.question: Is this safe for protected repositories?
faq.answer: Use normal repository protections and review rules. This skill does not replace branch protection or access control.
